title: Hazelnut truffles
subtitle: Dark chocolate ganache with toasted hazelnuts, rolled in cocoa
excerpt: Chocolate truffles made from a dark chocolate and cream ganache with chopped toasted hazelnuts folded in, chilled until firm, then rolled by hand into rough balls and dusted in cocoa powder. The ganache method is straightforward; the technique is in judging the right consistency for rolling.
sourceNotes: Chocolate truffles made from ganache are documented in French patisserie literature from the nineteenth century. The ganache method (hot cream over chopped chocolate) is a standard confectionery technique. The hazelnut variation follows established practice in European confectionery.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze rolled truffles on a tray, then bag once frozen. Thaw in the fridge overnight.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe; the ganache method scales straightforwardly.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Truffles keep in the fridge for 10 days or at room temperature in a cool place for 5 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: A truffle ganache is made at a ratio of roughly 2 parts chocolate to 1 part cream by weight. This produces a ganache that is liquid when warm and sets to a firm but yielding mass in the fridge, soft enough to scoop and roll but not so soft that it melts immediately in the hand. The ganache must be fully chilled before rolling; at room temperature it is too soft to hold a shape. Rolling is done quickly with lightly cocoa-dusted hands.

content.text: Hazelnut truffles are a ganache project. The

matters more than any other variable: too stiff and the truffle will be grainy and hard to roll; too soft and it will smear. The hazelnuts are toasted until the skins crack and the nut inside is golden; rubbed in a cloth to remove the skins, then chopped and folded into the ganache. The cocoa powder coating is traditional; finely chopped nuts or icing sugar are alternatives.
Toast the hazelnuts and make the ganache

Toast the hazelnuts in a dry frying pan over medium heat, shaking often, until the skins crack and the nuts are golden underneath, about 5 to 7 minutes. Tip onto a clean cloth, wrap, and rub vigorously to remove most of the skins. Chop roughly. Place the finely chopped dark chocolate in a heatproof bowl. Heat the double cream in a small saucepan until just coming to the boil. Pour the hot cream over the chocolate and leave for 1 minute without stirring. Then stir from the centre outward until the ganache is smooth and glossy. Add the butter and stir until melted. Fold in the chopped toasted hazelnuts. Leave to cool to room temperature, then refrigerate for at least 3 hours until firm.

Sift the cocoa powder into a shallow bowl. Scoop teaspoon-sized portions of the cold ganache and roll quickly between your palms into rough balls; the warmth of your hands will soften the ganache fast, so work quickly and return the tray to the fridge for 10 minutes if the ganache becomes too soft to shape. Drop each ball into the cocoa and roll to coat. Place on baking paper. Refrigerate until ready to serve.
